channel.title: Quantum Pulse
channel.description: A daily curated collection of the latest news, research, and developments in quantum computing, quantum physics, and quantum information science.

item.title: Boosted linear-optical measurements on single-rail qubits with unentangled ancillas
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a fundamental challenge in photonic quantum computing: how to perform arbitrary measurements on single photons when you can't easily make them interact with each other. The team demonstrates a clever workaround using unentangled ancilla photons and an 8-port interferometer to boost measurement success rates on single-rail qubits (where information is encoded in vacuum versus single-photon states) to nearly 60%, significantly improving on the previous 50% theoretical limit. This advance could be particularly impactful for quantum sensing applications and distributed quantum networks, where photons naturally serve as information carriers but their weak interactions have historically limited measurement capabilities. The approach represents an important step toward making photonic quantum systems more practical by squeezing better performance out of linear optical components.

Any quantum state of the radiation field, sliced in small non-overlapping space-time bins is a collection of single-rail qubits, each spanning the vacuum and single-photon Fock state of a mode. Quantum logic on these qubits would enable arbitrary measurements on information-bearing light, but is hard due to the lack of strong nonlinearities. With unentangled ancilla single-rail qubits, an $8$-port interferometer and photon detection, we show any single-rail qubit measurement in the $XY$ Bloch plane is realizable with success probability $147/256$, which beats the prior-known $1/2$ limit.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Decoherence and the Reemergence of Coherence From a Superconducting "Horizon"
item.description:
Curator's Take: This fascinating research demonstrates how superconducting circuits can serve as "quantum simulators" for some of the most exotic physics in the universe - the quantum effects near black hole event horizons. The team's discovery that decoherence from their artificial "horizon" can actually reverse and restore quantum coherence at strong coupling strengths suggests there might be previously unknown quantum phenomena occurring near real black holes that could be mediated by Hawking radiation. What makes this particularly exciting is that it opens up an entirely terrestrial laboratory approach to exploring the mysterious intersection of quantum mechanics and gravity, using accessible superconducting hardware rather than requiring actual black holes. This work exemplifies how quantum devices are becoming powerful tools not just for computation, but for probing fundamental physics questions that were previously purely theoretical.

In a recent paper arXiv:2205.06279, Danielson et al. demonstrated that the mere presence of a black hole causes universal decoherence of quantum superpositions (dubbed the DSW decoherence). This result has profound implications for the interplay of quantum mechanics and gravity. We analyze decoherence in a superconducting analogue arXiv:1709.06154 of the event horizon of a black hole, where Andreev reflection plays the role of Hawking radiation. We consider a normal metal interferometer threaded by an Aharonov-Bohm flux, where one of the arms of the interferometer is coupled to a superconductor by a tunnel coupling of varying strength. At absolute zero and for weak coupling, we find that the scattering states of the interferometer are decohered by Andreev reflection, a nontrivial manifestation of the proximity effect analogous to DSW decoherence from the event horizon of a black hole. However, for increasing coupling strength to the superconductor, we find a reemergence of coherence via resonant tunneling through Andreev bound states. This suggests the existence of an analogue gravitational phenomenon wherein transmission mediated by virtual Hawking radiation leads to a reemergence of coherence in an interferometer placed within a few Compton wavelengths of a black hole's event horizon. Our results open a new path to study black hole quantum physics on earth via analogue studies.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Secure Quantum Communication: Simulation and Analysis of Quantum Key Distribution Protocols
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive simulation study provides crucial insights into how quantum key distribution protocols perform under real-world conditions, moving beyond theoretical promises to examine practical challenges like noise and hardware limitations. By comparing three foundational QKD protocols (BB84, B92, and E91) using IBM's Qiskit framework, the research offers valuable benchmarking data for developers working to deploy quantum-secure communication systems. The work is particularly timely as organizations increasingly recognize the urgent need to prepare for post-quantum cryptography, with QKD representing one of the few provably secure alternatives to current encryption methods that will be vulnerable to future quantum computers. The study's focus on scalability challenges and channel losses addresses critical engineering hurdles that must be overcome before QKD can transition from laboratory demonstrations to widespread commercial deployment.

Quantum computing poses significant threats to conventional cryptographic techniques such as RSA and AES, motivating the need for quantum secure communication methods. Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) offers information theoretic security based on fundamental quantum principles. This paper presents a simulation-based analysis of well-known QKD protocols, namely BB84, B92, and E91, using the IBM Qiskit framework. Realistic quantum channel effects, including noise, decoherence, and eavesdropping, are modeled to evaluate protocol performance. Key metrics such as error rate, secret key generation, and security characteristics are analyzed and compared. The study highlights practical challenges in QKD implementation, including hardware limitations and channel losses, and discusses insights toward scalable and robust quantum communication systems. The results support the feasibility of QKD as a promising solution for secure communication in the quantum era.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: CryoCMOS RF multiplexer for superconducting qubit control, readout and flux biasing at millikelvin temperatures with picowatt power consumption
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough tackles one of the most pressing engineering challenges in scaling up quantum computers: the notorious "refrigerator wiring problem" where each qubit traditionally requires multiple dedicated control lines threading down from room temperature to the millikelvin quantum processor. By demonstrating a cryogenic CMOS multiplexer that operates directly at the qubit plane with incredibly low power consumption (just 200 picowatts), researchers have shown how to dramatically reduce the wiring complexity that currently limits quantum systems to hundreds rather than millions of qubits. The device's ability to handle readout, flux control, and drive signals while barely affecting qubit coherence times represents a crucial step toward the integrated quantum-classical control systems that large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computers will require. This work exemplifies the kind of quantum engineering innovation needed to bridge the gap between today's research devices and tomorrow's practical quantum processors.

Large-scale cryogenic quantum systems are constrained by an input-output bottleneck between room-temperature electronics and millikelvin stages, particularly in superconducting qubit platforms. This bottleneck is most acute for output lines, where bulky and expensive microwave components limit scalability. A promising approach for scalable characterization and testing is to perform signal multiplexing directly at the qubit plane. We demonstrate a cryogenic CMOS (cryoCMOS) RF multiplexer operating at 10 millikelvin with record-low static power consumption of 200 pW. The device provides < 2 dB insertion loss and > 30 dB isolation across DC-8 GHz. Direct connection to transmon qubits marginally affects coherence times in the range of 100 microseconds, enabling multiplexing of readout, flux and, in principle, XY drive lines. This work introduces cryoCMOS multiplexers as valuable tools for scalable, high-throughput cryogenic characterization and testing, and advances co-integrated quantum-classical control for future large-scale quantum processors.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Logarithmic-depth quantum state preparation of polynomials
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough addresses one of quantum computing's most fundamental bottlenecks by dramatically reducing the circuit depth needed to prepare polynomial quantum states from linear to logarithmic scaling—a game-changing improvement for near-term quantum devices where decoherence limits how deep circuits can be. The work is particularly significant because polynomial approximations are the backbone of scientific computing, from molecular simulations in quantum chemistry to financial modeling, making this a broadly applicable advance rather than a narrow theoretical result. The researchers' successful demonstration on a 14-qubit trapped-ion processor with over 500 gates proves the approach works in practice, not just on paper, while their novel techniques for implementing the linear combination of unitaries could have applications well beyond state preparation in the broader quantum algorithm toolkit.

Quantum state preparation is a central primitive in many quantum algorithms, yet it is generally resource intensive, with efficient constructions known only for structured families of states. This work introduces a method for preparing quantum states whose amplitudes are given by a degree$-d$ polynomial, using circuits with logarithmic depth in the number $n$ of qubits and only $\mathcal O(n)$ ancilla qubits, improving previous approaches that required linear-depth circuits. The construction first relies on a block-encoding of an affine diagonal operator based on its Pauli-basis decomposition, which involves only $n$ terms. A modified linear-combination-of-unitaries (LCU) technique is introduced to implement this decomposition in logarithmic depth, together with a novel circuit for the EXACT-one oracle that flags basis states in which exactly one qubit is in the state $|1\rangle$. It then uses a generalized quantum eigenvalue transformation (GQET) to promote this affine operator to an arbitrary degree polynomial. Theoretical analysis and numerical simulations are reported along with a proof-of-principle implementation on a trapped-ion quantum processor using $14$ qubits and more than $500$ primitive quantum gates. Because polynomial approximations are ubiquitous in scientific computing, this construction provides a scalable and resource-efficient approach to quantum state preparation, further improving the potential of quantum algorithms in fields such as chemistry, physics, engineering, and finance.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Dark state role in time-reversal symmetry breaking
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research reveals how "dark states" - quantum states that remain decoupled from driving fields - can serve as powerful tools for maintaining symmetry in quantum control protocols, even when full time-reversal symmetry is broken. The findings provide practical design principles for robust quantum operations by showing how these spectator states constrain system dynamics to more predictable, symmetric patterns in population transfer. This work has immediate relevance for quantum computing platforms built on atoms, molecules, and solid-state systems, where precise control over quantum state manipulation is essential for reliable gate operations and error correction schemes. The theoretical framework extends to arbitrary system sizes and offers quantum engineers new strategies for building more stable control protocols that could improve the fidelity of quantum processors.

We investigate the role of the global driving phase $Φ$ in the dynamics of driven few-level quantum systems, a central setting in coherent control of atomic, molecular, and solid-state platforms. In particular, we focus on systems with closed-loop couplings, where external driving fields induce interference effects that strongly influence population transfer and symmetry properties of time-evolution. While full time-reversal symmetry requires $Φ=0,π$, leading to a real Hamiltonian, we focus on a less restrictive transformation, the phase inversion (or complex conjugation of the Hamiltonian), under which population dynamics can remain symmetric even though coherences generally do not. We show that the presence of a dark (spectator) state is a sufficient condition for this population phase symmetry (P$Φ$S), as it constrains the dynamics to reduced subspaces characterized by SU(2) or open-loop SU(3) evolution. We analyze this mechanism in three- and four-level systems and derive general conditions for P$Φ$S that extend to generic $n$-level configurations, with $n$ even. These findings provide practical guidelines for achieving robust control in quantum systems, with potential applications in quantum information processing and quantum computing.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: High Fidelity Single-NV Qubit Quantum State Tomography by Photoelectric Readout
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical engineering challenge for room-temperature quantum computing by demonstrating that nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond can achieve exceptional quantum state readout fidelity (99.5%) using photoelectric detection instead of traditional optical methods. The breakthrough is significant because optical readout systems require bulky, complex optics that make it nearly impossible to scale NV-based quantum processors into practical, chip-sized devices that could integrate with conventional electronics. By proving that photoelectric readout maintains the same high fidelity as optical methods while being far more compatible with standard semiconductor manufacturing, this work removes a major obstacle to building ambient-temperature quantum computers that could operate outside of expensive dilution refrigerators. This development positions NV centers as increasingly viable candidates for distributed quantum networks and embedded quantum sensors that could revolutionize everything from medical imaging to navigation systems.

Quantum computing is a rapidly developing field. However, the most commonly used qubits require cryogenic conditions to operate, which increases the costs and puts constraints on the up-scaling. Ambient solid-state qubits provide an alternative with potential for large-scale application. The nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center in diamond is one of the main candidates for solid-state computing architectures at room temperature and has proven to be competitive in terms of gate fidelity, quantum error correction, couplings, etc. Each NV center has an associated electronic spin that is conventionally read out by photoluminescence. However, regarding the creation of small, ambient NV-based quantum processors, the optical readout introduces limitations on the collection efficiency and resolution of the readout as well as the size of the final device and its integration into standard semiconductor architectures. In this work, we investigate the competitiveness of the photoelectric readout versus the traditional optical readout. In particular, we report on using photoelectrical detection to perform quantum state tomography measurements on a single NV center. We achieve the fidelity $0.995 \pm 0.0062$ for state reconstruction, comparable to optical measurements, demonstrating that the fidelity does not suffer from the adapted readout, highlighting the value of photoelectric detection for NV-based quantum processors.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Distinguishing types of correlated errors in superconducting qubits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical challenge for scaling quantum computers: distinguishing between different types of correlated errors that can devastate quantum error correction schemes. The team's method for separating radiation-induced quasiparticle tunneling from mechanical vibration effects represents a significant step toward developing targeted mitigation strategies rather than generic approaches. Particularly intriguing is their finding that transmon designs with larger superconducting gap differences—already known to reduce radiation errors—also provide protection against vibration-induced errors, suggesting a unified hardware solution. This work could inform the design of more robust superconducting quantum processors by enabling engineers to identify and address specific error sources in their cryogenic environments.

Errors in superconducting qubits that are correlated in time and space can pose problems for quantum error correction codes. Radiation from cosmic and terrestrial sources can increase the quasiparticle (QP) density in a superconducting qubit device, resulting in an increased rate of QPs tunneling across proximal Josephson junctions (JJs) and causing correlated errors. Mechanical vibrations, such as those induced by the pulse tube in a dry dilution refrigerator, are also a known source of correlated errors. We present a method for distinguishing these two types of errors by their temporal, spatial, and frequency domain features, enabling physically motivated error-mitigation strategies. We also present accelerometer data to study the correlation between dilution refrigerator vibrations and the errors. We measure arrays of transmon qubits where the difference in superconducting gap across the JJ is less than the qubit energy, as well as those where the gap is greater than the qubit energy, which has been shown to mitigate radiation-induced errors. We show that these latter devices are also protected against vibration-induced errors.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Achieving Sub-Zeptonewton Force Sensitivity and Spin-Motion Entanglement in Levitated Diamond via Pulsed Backaction Evasion
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research pushes the boundaries of quantum sensing to unprecedented levels, achieving force sensitivity that's literally millions of times more precise than current state-of-the-art sensors—reaching into the sub-zeptonewton range (10^-21 Newtons). The breakthrough lies in cleverly using pulsed backaction evasion techniques with levitated nanodiamonds containing nitrogen-vacancy centers, which simultaneously creates robust spin-motion entanglement while beating the standard quantum limit that typically constrains precision measurements. Beyond the impressive sensitivity numbers, this work opens practical pathways for detecting incredibly weak forces like those predicted by theories of quantum gravity, dark matter interactions, or violations of the equivalence principle—making it a potential game-changer for both fundamental physics tests and next-generation sensing applications.

We propose a system to achieve sub-zeptonewton force sensing and robust spin-mechanical entanglement in a levitated diamond system. By coupling a Nitrogen-Vacancy (NV) center spin to the motion of its host diamond within a magnetic trap, we develop a platform designed to surpass the standard quantum limit. We develop and compare three distinct pulse sequences--Ramsey, Hahn echo, and Carr-Purcell-Meiboom-Gill (CPMG)--to create increasing amounts of backaction evasion while mitigating the effects of shot noise and thermal decoherence. Our results show that the CPMG sequences yield the most significant performance gains, reaching a force sensitivity of better than $10^{-23} \text{ N}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ for broadband sensing around $10^4 \text{ Hz}$. Furthermore, we derive an entanglement witness protocol that accounts for pulsed dynamical decoupling, proving that spin-motion entanglement remains detectable even when occurring much faster than the mechanical period. These findings provide a more practical path for using levitated nanodiamonds both as high-precision sensors and as non-classical mechanical systems for fundamental tests of quantum mechanics.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Chipmunq: Fault-Tolerant Compiler for Chiplet Quantum Architectures
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article addresses a critical bottleneck in scaling quantum computers: how to efficiently compile fault-tolerant quantum circuits for the modular chiplet architectures that will be essential for reaching millions of qubits. Chipmunq's breakthrough lies in understanding that traditional compilation approaches fall apart when dealing with quantum error correction codes spread across multiple chiplets connected by sparse, noisy links. The compiler's dramatic improvements—86% reduction in circuit depth and two orders of magnitude better logical error rates—could be the difference between theoretical fault-tolerance and practical quantum advantage. As companies like IBM and Google push toward modular quantum systems, tools like Chipmunq will be essential infrastructure for making these architectures actually work in practice.

As quantum computing advances toward fault-tolerance through quantum error correction, modular chiplet architectures have emerged to provide the massive qubit counts required while overcoming fabrication limits of monolithic chips. However, this transition introduces a critical compilation gap: existing frameworks cannot handle the scale of fault-tolerant quantum circuits while managing the noisy, sparse interconnects of chiplet backends. We present Chipmunq, the first hardware-aware compiler for mapping and routing fault-tolerant circuits onto modular architectures. Chipmunq employs a quantum-error-correction-aware partitioning strategy that preserves the integrity of logical qubit patches, preventing prohibitive gate overheads common in general-purpose compilers. Our evaluation demonstrates that Chipmunq achieves a 13.5x speedup in compilation time compared to state-of-the-art tools. By incorporating chiplet constraints and defective qubits, it reduces circuit depth by 86.4% and SWAP gate counts by 91.4% across varying code distances. Crucially, Chipmunq overcomes heterogeneous inter-chiplet links, improving logical error rates by up to two orders of magnitude.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: How Quantum Circuits Actually Learn: A Causal Identification of Genuine Quantum Contributions
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum machine learning's most pressing questions: are performance improvements actually coming from quantum effects, or just from having more parameters and clever circuit design? Using sophisticated causal analysis across multiple circuit types and datasets, the authors found that genuine quantum contributions (like entanglement) account for less than 1% of performance gains, while classical architectural factors dominate by a ratio of 13:1. While this might sound disappointing, it's actually valuable news—it means current quantum ML algorithms are operating far below their quantum potential and provides a clear roadmap for designing circuits that genuinely leverage quantum resources rather than just mimicking classical neural networks with quantum hardware.

Attributing performance gains in quantum machine learning to genuine quantum resources rather than to classical architectural scaling remains an open methodological challenge. We address this by introducing a counterfactual causal mediation framework that decomposes inter-architectural performance differences into direct effects, attributable to circuit parameterization and expressivity, and indirect effects mediated by quantum information-theoretic observables: entanglement entropy, purity, linear entropy, and quantum mutual information. Applying this framework to five circuit topologies and three benchmark datasets (across 43 validated configurations) reveals that direct architectural contributions systematically exceed quantum-mediated effects, with a mean ratio of 13.1:1 and a mean indirect contribution of 0.82%. These results suggest that current variational quantum circuits operate substantially below their quantum potential, and that principled resource-aware circuit design represents a tractable path toward measurable quantum-mediated performance gains.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: An Energetic Constraint for Qubit-Qubit Entanglement
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research unveils a fundamental energetic principle that could reshape how we think about generating and preserving quantum entanglement in practical systems. By demonstrating that creating entanglement between qubits necessarily comes at the cost of individual qubit coherent energy—with this "energy deficit" precisely quantifying the entanglement strength—the work provides a new lens for understanding the physical resources required for quantum operations. The finding that this energy trade-off equals the square negativity, a well-established entanglement measure, creates an elegant bridge between thermodynamics and quantum information theory that could inform more energy-efficient quantum computing architectures. For quantum engineers working with limited energy budgets, this framework offers concrete metrics to optimize entanglement protocols while accounting for the fundamental energetic costs of quantum coherence.

We analyze qubit-qubit entanglement from an energetic perspective and reveal an energetic trade-off between quantum coherence and entanglement. We decompose each qubit internal energy into a coherent and an incoherent component. The qubits' coherent energies are maximal if the qubit-qubit state is pure and separable. They decrease as qubit-qubit entanglement builds up under locally-energy-preserving processes. This yields a "coherent energy deficit" that we show is equal to a well-known measure of entanglement, the square negativity. In general, a qubit-qubit state can always be represented as a mixture of pure states. Then, the coherent energy deficit splits into a quantum component, corresponding to the average square negativity of the pure states, and a classical one reflecting the mixedness of the joint state. Minimizing the quantum deficit over the possible pure state decompositions yields the square negativity of the mixture. Our findings bring out new figures of merit to optimize and secure entanglement generation and distribution under energetic constraints.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Towards End-to-End Quantum Estimation of Non-Hermitian Pseudospectra
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a fundamental challenge in quantum many-body physics by developing the first complete quantum algorithm for analyzing pseudospectra—a crucial tool for understanding how non-Hermitian quantum systems respond to perturbations. The work is particularly significant because it bridges pure theory with practical implementation, demonstrating their "QSIGS" algorithm on actual trapped-ion hardware to distinguish spectral stability regions around exceptional points. What makes this especially compelling is the computational complexity result showing that pseudospectrum problems are naturally suited for quantum advantage, with the classical version being PSPACE-hard while remaining in QMA for quantum computers. This represents a rare example of moving from fundamental complexity theory to working quantum algorithms that could help predict and control the behavior of open quantum systems and non-Hermitian materials.

Non-Hermitian many-body systems can be spectrally unstable, so small perturbations may induce large eigenvalue shifts. The pseudospectrum quantifies this instability and provides a perturbation-robust diagnostic. For inverse-polynomially small $ε$, we show that deciding whether a point $z\in\mathbb{C}$ is $ε$-close to the spectrum is PSPACE-hard for $5$-local operators, whereas deciding whether $z$ lies in the $ε$-pseudospectrum is QMA-complete for $4$-local operators. This identifies pseudospectrum membership as a natural computational target. We then present a concrete end-to-end quantum framework for deciding pseudospectrum membership, which combines a singular-value estimation step with a dissipative state preparation algorithm. Our Quantum Singular-value Gaussian-filtered Search (QSIGS) combines quantum singular value transformation (QSVT) with classical post-processing to achieve Heisenberg-limited query scaling for singular-value estimation. To prepare suitable input states, we introduce an algorithmic Lindbladian protocol for approximate ground right singular vectors and prove its effectiveness for the Hatano--Nelson model. Finally, we demonstrate the full pipeline on a trapped-ion quantum computer and distinguish points inside and outside the target pseudospectrum near the exceptional point of a minimal non-Hermitian qubit model.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: A Scalable Open-Source QEC System with Sub-Microsecond Decoding-Feedback Latency
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum computing's most pressing engineering bottlenecks: building the ultra-fast error correction systems needed for fault-tolerant quantum computers. While much attention focuses on clever decoding algorithms, this team demonstrates that real-world quantum error correction requires seamless integration of control systems, networking, and decoders—achieving an impressive 446 nanosecond end-to-end latency that's fast enough to outpace error accumulation in superconducting qubits. The open-source nature of their RISC-Q platform could accelerate industry adoption by providing a proven hardware foundation that scales to distance-21 surface codes, bringing us closer to the thousands of logical qubits needed for practical quantum algorithms.

Quantum error correction (QEC) is essential for realizing large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computation, yet its practical implementation remains a major engineering challenge. In particular, QEC demands precise real-time control of a large number of qubits and low-latency, high-throughput and accurate decoding of error syndromes. While most prior work has focused primarily on decoder design, the overall performance of any QEC system depends critically on all its subsystems including control, communication, and decoding, as well as their integration. To address this challenge, we present an open-source, fully integrated QEC system built on RISC-Q, a generator for RISC-V-based quantum control architectures. Implemented on RFSoC FPGAs, our system prototype integrates real-time qubit control, a scalable distributed multi-board architecture, and the state-of-the-art hardware QEC decoder within a low-latency, high-throughput decoding pipeline, forming a complete hardware platform ready for deployment with superconducting qubits. Experimental evaluation on a three-board prototype based on AMD ZCU216 RFSoCs demonstrates an end-to-end QEC decoding-feedback latency of 446 ns for a distance-3 surface code, including syndrome aggregation, network communication, syndrome decoding, and error distribution. Extrapolating from measured subsystem performance and state-of-the-art decoder benchmarks, the architecture can achieve sub-microsecond decoding-feedback latency up to a distance-21 surface code ($\sim$881 physical qubits) when scaled to larger hardware configurations.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Qudit Implementation of the Rodeo Algorithm for Quantum Spectral Filtering
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research demonstrates how qudits—quantum systems with more than two levels—can significantly improve the performance of quantum algorithms, achieving an 18% reduction in measurement fluctuations compared to traditional qubit implementations. The work introduces an elegant "Rodeo kernel" concept that acts as a spectral filter, potentially making quantum simulations of complex materials and molecules more precise and efficient. By extending beyond the binary qubit paradigm, this approach opens new pathways for quantum advantage in computational chemistry and condensed matter physics, where accurately capturing multi-level quantum systems is crucial. The microcanonical protocol for estimating thermodynamic properties through a single energy sweep could prove particularly valuable for studying phase transitions and quantum many-body systems.

Qudits, the multi-level generalization of qubits, provide a natural extension of the binary paradigm in quantum computation and offer new opportunities to enhance algorithmic performance. Beyond their direct applicability to the simulation of multi-level quantum systems, higher-dimensional ancillae can improve sampling efficiency in quantum algorithms by enabling the simultaneous implementation of multiple control operations, thereby reducing circuit complexity. In this work, we pursue three main objectives. First, we present a formulation of the Rodeo algorithm employing a general $d$-level ancilla qudit. Second, we introduce the concept of the \emph{Rodeo kernel}, defined as a two-frequency interferometer, which acts as a spectral filter in the energy domain. Finally, we propose a microcanonical protocol for the Rodeo algorithm. This protocol enables the estimation of entropic quantities through a single energy sweep and admits a natural interpretation as a Gaussian convolution of the density of states. To support the theoretical analysis, we perform numerical evaluations of the corresponding quantum circuit using ancilla qudits of dimensions three, four, and five. The simulations are performed for the one-dimensional Ising model, considering both spin-$\frac{1}{2}$ and spin-$1$ particles. The ancilla qutrit implementation exhibits an $18\%$ reduction in fluctuations compared to the qubit implementation. Our results show that the qudits provide a framework for spectral analysis and thermodynamic characterization of multi-level quantum systems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Monolithic Segmented 3D Ion Trap for Quantum Technology Applications
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a significant step toward scalable ion trap quantum computing by solving a persistent engineering challenge: how to maintain the precise control needed for high-fidelity operations while working with heavy ions like Ytterbium and Barium. The monolithic 3D fused silica design cleverly combines the manufacturing precision of chip-scale traps with the performance advantages of larger, traditional ion traps, achieving an impressive 99.3% two-qubit gate fidelity and ultralow heating rates that have historically plagued miniaturized systems. What makes this particularly exciting is the "segmented" architecture that enables modular scaling—imagine connecting multiple trap modules like quantum Lego blocks to build larger processors. The demonstration with heavy ionic species is especially important since these ions offer unique advantages for quantum networking and atomic clocks, potentially opening new pathways for distributed quantum systems and precision metrology applications.

Monolithic three-dimensional (3D) Paul traps combine the high-precision microfabrication of two-dimensional (2D) chip traps with the deep trapping potentials and low heating rates characteristic of macroscopic Paul traps, which are typically manually assembled. However, achieving low motional heating rates and optical access with a high numerical aperture (NA) while maintaining the high radio-frequency (RF) voltages required for heavy ionic species, such as Yb$^{+}$ and Ba$^{+}$, remains a significant technical challenge. In this work, we present a segmented, monolithic 3D fused silica blade trap, featuring an ion-electrode distance of 250 $μ$m with stable operation at high RF voltages. We benchmark the performance of the trap using Yb$^{+}$ ions, demonstrating axially homogeneous trapping potentials for 200 $μ$m around the axial center of the trap, high multi-directional optical access (up to 0.7 NA), and radial motional heating rate as low as 1.1 $\pm$ 0.1 quanta/s at radial trap frequencies about 3 MHz near room temperature. Furthermore, we observe a motional Ramsey coherence time, $T_{2}$, of around 95 ms for the radial center-of-mass mode. We demonstrate a two-qubit gate fidelity of ${99.3}^{+ 0.7}_{- 1.5}$$\%$ with state preparation and measurement correction. These results establish fused-silica monolithic blade traps as a scalable, modular platform for quantum simulation, computation, metrology, and networking with heavy ionic species.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: 3D tomography of exchange phase in a Si/SiGe quantum dot device
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of the most persistent challenges in silicon quantum dot spin qubits: accurately measuring and mapping the exchange interaction that enables two-qubit gates across different voltage configurations. The researchers have developed an innovative 3D tomography approach that borrows techniques from digital holography and network flow algorithms to robustly extract phase information from noisy experimental data—a critical step toward precise qubit calibration and control. This work represents a significant methodological advance for silicon quantum processors, as understanding how exchange interactions vary with gate voltages is essential for scaling up these systems and achieving the high-fidelity operations needed for fault-tolerant quantum computing. The ability to create detailed 3D maps of exchange phases could dramatically improve how researchers characterize and optimize spin qubit devices, potentially accelerating the development of large-scale silicon quantum computers.

The exchange interaction is a foundational building block for the operation of spin-based quantum processors. Extracting the exchange interaction coefficient $J(\mathbf{V})$, as a function of gate electrode voltages, is important for understanding disorder, faithfully simulating device performance, and operating spin qubits with high fidelity. Typical coherent measurements of exchange in spin qubit devices yield a modulated cosine of an accumulated phase, which in turn is the time integral of exchange. As such, extracting $J(\mathbf{V})$ from experimental data is difficult due to the ambiguity of inverting a cosine, the sensitivity to noise when unwrapping phase, as well as the problem of inverting the integral. As a step toward obtaining $J(\mathbf{V})$, we tackle the first two challenges to reveal the accumulated phase, $φ(\mathbf{V})$. We incorporate techniques from a wide range of fields to robustly extract and model a 3D phase volume for spin qubit devices from a sequence of 2D measurements. In particular, we present a measurement technique to obtain the wrapped phase, as done in phase-shifting digital holography, and utilize the max-flow/min-cut phase unwrapping method (PUMA) to unwrap the phase in 3D voltage space. We show this method is robust to the minimal observed drift in the device, which we confirm by increasing scan resolution. Upon building a model of the extracted phase, we optimize over the model to locate a minimal-gradient $π$ exchange pulse point in voltage space. Our measurement protocol may provide detailed information useful for understanding the origins of device variability governing device yield, enable calibrating device models to specific devices during operation for more sophisticated error attribution, and enable a systematic optimization of qubit control. We anticipate that the methods presented here may be applicable to other qubit platforms.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computing meets the Möbius molecule
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a fascinating convergence where quantum computing hardware is finally sophisticated enough to tackle complex molecular structures like the Möbius molecule, which features unusual twisted geometries and intricate electron interactions that have long challenged classical computational methods. The ability to simulate such multi-electron systems represents a crucial milestone for quantum chemistry applications, as these Möbius structures appear in everything from advanced materials to biological processes. What makes this particularly exciting is that it demonstrates how today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices are moving beyond toy problems toward real chemical systems that could inform drug discovery and materials science, bridging the gap between quantum computing's theoretical promise and practical molecular modeling.

A complex structure with multiple electrons is within reach of today's hardware.

Read original at Ars Technica - Science
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: A tiny light trap could unlock million qubit quantum computers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This Stanford breakthrough tackles one of quantum computing's most stubborn scaling challenges: efficiently reading out information from large arrays of qubits simultaneously. While current quantum computers struggle with the complex infrastructure needed to control and measure even hundreds of qubits, these miniature optical cavities could dramatically simplify the readout process by allowing parallel measurement of many atomic qubits at once. The leap from demonstrating dozens to hundreds of cavities suggests the approach has real scaling potential, addressing a critical bottleneck that has limited quantum computers to the hundreds-of-qubits range. If this optical readout method proves robust, it could be a key enabling technology for the million-qubit quantum computers needed for truly transformative applications like drug discovery and climate modeling.

A new light-based breakthrough could help quantum computers finally scale up. Stanford researchers created miniature optical cavities that efficiently collect light from individual atoms, allowing many qubits to be read at once. The team has already demonstrated working arrays with dozens and even hundreds of cavities. The approach could eventually support massive quantum networks with millions of qubits.

Read original at Science Daily - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: “Purifying” photons: Scientists found a way to clean light itself
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough tackles one of photonic quantum computing's most persistent challenges: the "dirty" light that typically sabotages delicate quantum operations. By turning the problem on its head and using unwanted photons as a cleaning mechanism rather than just filtering them out, the University of Iowa team has found an elegant solution that could dramatically improve the quality of single-photon sources. This approach is particularly exciting because it works with existing laser technology, potentially making high-purity photonic qubits more accessible without requiring entirely new hardware architectures. The advancement could accelerate progress in both quantum computing and quantum cryptography, where pristine single photons are absolutely essential for maintaining quantum states and ensuring security.

A new discovery shows that messy, stray light can be used to clean up quantum systems instead of disrupting them. University of Iowa researchers found that unwanted photons produced by lasers can be canceled out by carefully tuning the light itself. The result is a much purer stream of single photons, a key requirement for quantum computing and secure communication. The work could help push photonic quantum technology closer to real-world use.

Read original at Science Daily - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly
